# Durham Timetable to ICS Converter

## Project Overview

This project will provide a browser-based tool that converts Durham University timetable exports into standard iCalendar (`.ics`) files suitable for import into Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar, and other calendar applications.

The system will be implemented as a fully static web application hosted on GitHub Pages (or equivalent static hosting). No backend services, databases, authentication systems, or cloud infrastructure are required for normal operation.

The application will parse Durham timetable HTML exports, combine them with academic week definitions, expand timetable occurrences into dated events, optimise recurring events where appropriate, and generate a downloadable `.ics` file.

A secondary maintenance tool will assist in generating academic year definitions from Durham's published week converter page.

---

# Objectives

The application should:

1. Accept Durham timetable HTML exports.
2. Parse timetable entries and associated metadata.
3. Convert Durham week numbers into calendar dates.
4. Generate standards-compliant iCalendar files.
5. Support recurring events where appropriate.
6. Support optional calendar reminders.
7. Operate entirely within the browser.
8. Require no server-side infrastructure.
9. Be maintainable with minimal annual effort.

---

# Design Principles

## Static-First

All timetable processing should occur locally within the browser.

Benefits:

* Free hosting.
* No infrastructure maintenance.
* No authentication.
* No privacy concerns associated with uploading timetable data.
* Long-term sustainability.

## Durham-Specific

The application is designed specifically for Durham University timetable exports.

The implementation should leverage the known structure of Durham timetable HTML rather than attempting to support arbitrary timetable systems.

## Deterministic Parsing

The timetable exports are machine-generated and highly structured.

Parsing should rely on explicit structural rules rather than heuristics or AI-based extraction.

---

# System Architecture

## Main Application

```text
Timetable HTML
       ↓
Timetable Parser
       ↓
Activity Objects
       ↓
Week Expansion
       ↓
Date Generation
       ↓
Recurrence Optimisation
       ↓
ICS Generator
       ↓
Download File
```

All processing occurs client-side.

---

## Academic Year Definitions

Academic year definitions are stored in a single JSON file.

Example:

```json
{
  "currentYear": "2026-27",

  "years": {
    "2025-26": {
      "week0Start": "2025-07-21",
      "michaelmasStart": 11,
      "epiphanyStart": 26,
      "easterStart": 41
    },

    "2026-27": {
      "week0Start": "2026-07-20",
      "michaelmasStart": 11,
      "epiphanyStart": 26,
      "easterStart": 41
    }
  }
}
```

The application loads this file at startup.

No database is required.

---

# Inputs

## Timetable HTML

Primary workflow:

```text
User downloads timetable page
          ↓
Uploads HTML file
          ↓
Application processes file
```

Future enhancement:

* Paste HTML directly.
* Drag-and-drop upload.

Direct URL import is explicitly out of scope for Version 1.

---

## Academic Year Definitions

Academic year definitions are derived from Durham's:

```text
week_patterns.htm
```

page.

Definitions are maintained separately from timetable processing.

---

# Maintenance Tool

A secondary static page will assist with annual updates.

Workflow:

```text
Upload week_patterns.htm
           ↓
Extract parameters
           ↓
Generate JSON fragment
           ↓
Download or copy JSON
           ↓
Add to weekmaps.json
```

This tool is intended for maintainers rather than end users.

No online storage is required.

---

# Data Model

## Activity

Each timetable entry should be parsed into a structured object.

Example:

```javascript
{
  moduleCode: "MATH2761",
  moduleName: "Statistical Inference II",

  activityTypeCode: "LECT",
  activityType: "Lecture",

  group: "001",

  room: "CG93",

  staff: [
    "Cumming, Dr J A"
  ],

  day: "Tuesday",

  startTime: "14:00",
  endTime: "16:00",

  weeks: "12-16"
}
```

---

# Activity Type Decoding

Durham timetable activity codes should be translated into human-readable labels.

Examples:

| Code  | Label         |
| ----- | ------------- |
| LECT  | Lecture       |
| TUT   | Tutorial      |
| PRAC  | Practical     |
| SEM   | Seminar       |
| WORK  | Workshop      |
| LAB   | Laboratory    |
| PROB  | Problem Class |
| PROBA | Problem Class |
| PROBB | Problem Class |

Unknown activity codes should not cause failure.

The application should display the original code if no mapping exists.

The mapping should be maintained in a separate configuration file to allow future updates.

---

# Week Pattern Parsing

The Durham timetable uses week specifications such as:

```text
12-16
```

```text
17-21
```

```text
14,16-17,19,21
```

```text
26-29,31-34
```

The parser must expand these into explicit week numbers.

Examples:

```text
12-16
```

becomes:

```text
12,13,14,15,16
```

and

```text
14,16-17,19,21
```

becomes:

```text
14,16,17,19,21
```

---

# Date Generation

Academic week numbers are converted into calendar dates using:

```text
week0Start
```

from the selected academic year definition.

The start date for each week is calculated from:

```text
week0Start + (7 × weekNumber days)
```

The timetable weekday is then applied.

---

# Calendar Event Naming

Generated calendar events should be understandable without opening them.

Default title format:

```text
Statistical Inference II — Lecture
```

Where necessary:

```text
Statistical Inference II — Tutorial 001
```

The title should prioritise:

1. Module name
2. Activity type
3. Group identifier

Module codes should generally be placed in the description rather than the title.

---

# Event Description

The DESCRIPTION field should contain richer information.

Example:

```text
Module: MATH2761
Activity: Lecture
Group: 001

Staff:
Cumming, Dr J A

Room:
CG93

Weeks:
12-16
```

This keeps calendar titles concise while preserving useful information.

---

# Event Location

The LOCATION field should contain the timetable room.

Example:

```text
CG93
```

This allows calendar applications to display location information separately from the title.

---

# Recurrence Optimisation

The application should use recurring events whenever this produces a correct representation.

## Weekly Patterns

Example:

```text
12-16
```

should become a recurring event:

```text
RRULE:FREQ=WEEKLY;COUNT=5
```

rather than five separate events.

## Fortnightly Patterns

Example:

```text
12,14,16,18,20
```

should become:

```text
RRULE:FREQ=WEEKLY;INTERVAL=2;COUNT=5
```

## Irregular Patterns

Example:

```text
14,16-17,19,21,41
```

should be expanded into separate events.

The recurrence engine should:

1. Expand week patterns.
2. Detect regular weekly sequences.
3. Detect regular fortnightly sequences.
4. Fall back to explicit events when necessary.

This reduces calendar size while preserving correctness.

---

# Calendar Alerts

The application should support optional reminders.

Suggested options:

* None
* 10 minutes before
* 15 minutes before
* 30 minutes before
* 1 hour before
* 1 day before

These should be implemented using standard iCalendar VALARM entries.

Example:

```text
BEGIN:VALARM
ACTION:DISPLAY
DESCRIPTION:Reminder
TRIGGER:-PT15M
END:VALARM
```

Alert settings should apply globally to all generated events.

Per-event reminders are out of scope.

---

# Calendar Metadata

Generated calendars should include metadata such as:

```text
X-WR-CALNAME:Durham Teaching Timetable
```

This may improve presentation in some calendar clients.

Behaviour remains client-dependent.

---

# Calendar Selection

The application cannot reliably determine which calendar receives imported events.

The destination calendar is controlled by the target application.

Examples:

* Google Calendar allows the user to choose a destination calendar during import.
* Outlook provides its own import workflow.
* Apple Calendar generally imports into the selected calendar.

The application may suggest a calendar name but cannot enforce calendar placement.

---

# User Interface

## Main Converter

Inputs:

* Upload timetable HTML

Options:

* Academic year selection
* Reminder selection
* Compact title mode
* Verbose title mode

Outputs:

* Event count
* Preview table
* Download ICS button

---

## Title Modes

### Compact

```text
Statistical Inference II — Lecture
```

### Verbose

```text
MATH2761 Statistical Inference II — Lecture (Group 001)
```

Users may choose their preferred style.

---

## Event Preview

Before download, the application should display a preview table showing:

* Date
* Start time
* End time
* Event title
* Location

This allows users to verify the generated calendar before import.

---

# Staleness Detection

The application should warn when academic year definitions appear out of date.

Example:

```text
Latest available year: 2026-27

Warning:
Academic year definitions may be out of date.
```

This warning should not prevent operation.

---

# Error Handling

The application should detect and report:

* Unknown activity type codes.
* Missing academic year definitions.
* Unparseable week expressions.
* Invalid timetable structures.
* Ambiguous recurrence patterns.

Warnings should be presented to the user but should not normally prevent calendar generation.

---

# Project Structure

```text
/
├── index.html
├── weekmaps.json
│
├── css/
│   └── styles.css
│
├── js/
│   ├── timetable-parser.js
│   ├── activity-types.js
│   ├── week-parser.js
│   ├── date-generator.js
│   ├── recurrence.js
│   ├── ics-generator.js
│   └── app.js
│
├── admin/
│   ├── index.html
│   └── admin.js
│
└── README.md
```

---

# Version 1 Scope

Version 1 should include:

* Static hosting.
* Timetable HTML upload.
* Timetable parsing.
* Activity type decoding.
* Week pattern expansion.
* Academic year definitions loaded from JSON.
* Date generation.
* Recurrence optimisation.
* Optional reminders.
* Event preview.
* ICS generation.
* Downloadable calendar.
* Maintenance tool for annual week-definition updates.

No backend services, databases, cloud storage, authentication systems, or server-side processing are required.

---

# Future Enhancements

Potential future developments include:

* Direct URL import.
* Drag-and-drop upload.
* Paste HTML support.
* Room timetable support.
* Module timetable support.
* Duplicate detection.
* Event filtering.
* Calendar category assignment where supported.
* Automatic academic year detection.
* Direct generation of JSON files from Durham week converter exports.

These enhancements should not influence the Version 1 architecture.

# Specification Amendments Following Design Review

## Academic Year Selection

The application shall attempt to determine the academic year automatically from the uploaded timetable and select the corresponding academic year definition.

Users shall be able to override the detected academic year via a dropdown selector.

## Time Zone Handling

All generated calendar events shall use the Europe/London time zone.

Event times shall therefore correctly account for British Summer Time transitions where applicable.

## Event Naming

The application shall support the following event title formats:

### Full Module Names

```
Statistical Inference II: Lecture
Statistical Inference II: Tutorial
Statistical Inference II: Problems Class
```

### Short Module Names

```
SI2: Lecture
SI2: Tutorial
SI2: Problems Class
```

The user shall be able to choose between Full and Short naming modes.

Short module names shall be maintained in the academic-year configuration data.

Module codes such as `MATH2761` shall not be included in event titles.

## Activity Type Mapping

The application shall translate timetable activity codes into human-readable activity names.

Initial mappings:

| Code  | Display Name   |
| ----- | -------------- |
| LECT  | Lecture        |
| TUT   | Tutorial       |
| PRAC  | Practical      |
| PROB  | Problems Class |
| PROBA | Problems Class |

Unknown activity codes shall fall back to the original code value.

## Event Location

The room value from the timetable shall be exported using the ICS `LOCATION` field.

Example:

```
LOCATION:CG93
```

## Event Description

Generated calendar events shall contain additional information in the event description.

Suggested structure:

```
Module: Statistical Inference II
Module Code: MATH2761
Activity: Lecture
Room: CG93
Staff: Cumming, Dr J A
Timetable Entry: MATH2761/LECT/001
```

The exact formatting may vary provided the information is retained.

## Recurrence Optimisation

The recurrence engine shall attempt to minimise the number of calendar entries while preserving exact dates.

The optimisation strategy shall be:

1. Continuous weekly sequences become weekly recurrence rules.
2. Alternating-week sequences become fortnightly recurrence rules.
3. Irregular week patterns shall be split into the smallest sensible recurring groups.
4. Remaining isolated occurrences shall be emitted as individual events.

Correctness shall take priority over maximum compression.

## Event UID Generation

Generated ICS events shall use deterministic UIDs derived from timetable content.

Repeated exports of the same timetable should therefore update existing events in calendar clients rather than creating duplicates.

## Week Calendar Export

An optional secondary ICS file shall be available.

This file shall contain all-day events marking the start of teaching weeks.

Examples:

```
Week 12
Week 13
Week 14
```

Events shall be created on the Monday corresponding to each teaching week.

### Optional Term Markers

The week calendar may additionally include all-day events marking the end of each teaching term.

Examples:

```
End of Michaelmas Term
End of Epiphany Term
End of Easter Term
```

The term-end dates shall be derived from the academic-year definition data.

### Export Behaviour

The week-marker calendar shall be generated as a separate ICS file rather than being merged with teaching events.

This allows users to subscribe to, import, or remove the week calendar independently.

## Validation Summary

After processing, the application shall display a processing summary.

Example:

```
Activities parsed: 14
Calendar events generated: 52
Recurring series: 8
Single events: 6
Warnings: 0
```

This summary is intended to assist users when validating generated output.
